petitioner_background: The petitioner is a vice president and quantitative analyst at a financial institution (name redacted), where he has worked in quantitative analysis since 2009 and in his current role since 2016. He sought classification as an alien of extraordinary ability under EB-1A (INA § 203(b)(1)(A)), specializing in emerging markets trading and risk management. The same employer had previously filed a separate EB-2 advanced degree petition on his behalf, which was approved with a priority date of December 23, 2015.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner wrote or co-wrote several pieces for his employer's platform, including Market Commentaries, Strategy Weekly, and Strategy Monthly newsletters. The Market Commentaries discuss rates and prices relating to international financial products and market trends, while the Strategy Weekly newsletters discuss strategies, methods, and market performance. The Market Commentaries were marked 'Intended for Institutional clients only' and the strategy documents were marked 'For Institutional Investors Only - Not for Onward Distribution.' The petitioner submitted a statement from the global head of the employer's Wire stating that these materials are 'not available to the general public because they contain valuable proprietary analysis intended to provide a strategic advantage to [the employer's] clients.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO agreed that the writings qualified as scholarly in nature, finding that the materials are written in technical and specialized language not consistently accessible to lay readers, and therefore were written for learned persons in the field. However, the AAO found the articles were not published in professional or major trade publications or other major media because they were restricted to institutional clients only and not available to the general public or to 'all persons having profound knowledge of [his] field.' The AAO held that the petitioner and his employer both stipulated the writings are not available to the field at large, and by design are kept out of the hands of most others in the industry, so they cannot have field-wide impact or the same effect as scholarly journals which exist to disseminate knowledge. The AAO concluded that limited distribution is not the same as publication, and declined to address whether the writings were scholarly because the publication requirement was not met.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed several original business-related contributions of major significance in emerging markets trading and risk management, including: (1) developing novel software for efficiently running his employer's proprietary prepayment model; (2) demonstrating the profitability of investing in the Colombian peso through risk decomposition; (3) single-handedly running his employer's investments in Brazil during the Brazilian financial crisis, generating profits for institutional clients while most businesses were losing money in Brazil; and (4) developing a model of Argentine hybrid bond pricing enabling investors to manage risk of investing in these novel bonds. The petitioner asserted his contributions had widespread impact on emerging markets trading and risk management, directing risk management and investment/trading decisions of thousands of U.S. institutions, claiming his work 'increased the stability and liquidity of Colombia's currency' and 'advanced the Argentine emerging market.' He submitted four letters discussing specific claimed contributions (with other letters offering more general praise), and asserted he disseminated his contributions through the written pieces. Three letters (attributed to the head of Trading and Sales, the executive director of Emerging Markets at a bank, and the managing director of Emerging Markets Trading) discussed the same six contributions in the same order using similar/identical wording. A fourth letter from the managing director and head of Latin America Trading discussed eight contributions in a different order but with similar language.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that the limited distribution of the petitioner's work also limits the scope of his impact on the field. The Director had determined the petitioner's work was significant to his employer and its clients, but not to the field as a whole. The AAO gave reduced evidentiary weight to the letters due to striking similarities in structure and wording — three letters discussed the same six contributions in the same order with similar or identical language (illustrated by nearly identical passages about Argentine hybrid bonds), citing Surinder Singh v. BIA and Mei Chai Ye v. U.S. Dep't of Justice for the principle that strikingly similar affidavits may indicate a common source. The AAO noted that per Matter of Caron Int'l, expert letters are not presumptive evidence of eligibility and USCIS may give less weight to opinions that are not corroborated or are questionable. The AAO found that while the petitioner argued his proprietary work product could not reasonably be distributed beyond his employer and clients, and that benefit to the employer ultimately benefits the broader economy, he did not cite specific figures or submit documentary evidence establishing the extent to which his work has had field-wide impact. The AAO concluded the petitioner had not established his contributions were of major significance in the field.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the petitioner failed to submit the required initial evidence of either a one-time achievement or documentation meeting at least three of the ten regulatory criteria. The Director had found the petitioner met only two criteria (leading/critical role and high remuneration), and the AAO agreed, rejecting the petitioner's claims under the scholarly articles and original contributions criteria. Because the initial evidence threshold was not met, the AAO did not need to conduct a final merits determination under Kazarian, but noted it had reviewed the record in the aggregate and concluded it did not support a finding that the petitioner established the sustained national or international acclaim and recognition required for the classification. The AAO also observed that had the matter proceeded to a final merits determination, the petitioner would have had to explain how he achieved sustained national or international acclaim when his work was deliberately limited in distribution.
legal_citations: Silverman v. Eastrich Multiple Investor Fund, L.P., 51 F.3d 28 (3rd Cir. 1995)
Matter of Caron Int'l, 19 I&N Dec. 791 (Comm'r 1988)
Surinder Singh v. BIA, 438 F.3d 145 (2d Cir. 2006)
Mei Chai Ye v. U.S. Dep't of Justice, 489 F.3d 517 (2d Cir. 2007)
USCIS Policy Memorandum PM-602-0005.1, Evaluation of Evidence Submitted with Certain Form I-140 Petitions (Dec. 22, 2010)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The AAO identified credibility concerns with the petitioner's expert letters, finding that three letters discussed the same six contributions in the same order using similar and at times identical wording, and a fourth letter used similar language for related claims. The AAO cited case law from the asylum context (Surinder Singh v. BIA and Mei Chai Ye v. U.S. Dep't of Justice) suggesting that strikingly similar affidavits may indicate a common source, and reduced the evidentiary weight accorded to these letters. No formal fraud finding was made.
